documents.content: Amazon interviews revolve around the Leadership Principles (LPs) more than any other FAANG. You will be evaluated against the 16 LPs by every interviewer, and each interviewer 'owns' 2-3 LPs they probe deeply. The 6 LPs that come up most often: Customer Obsession (start with the customer and work backwards), Ownership (think long-term, don't sacrifice long-term value for short-term results), Bias for Action (speed matters; reversible decisions can be made fast), Deliver Results (focus on key inputs and deliver with the right quality), Earn Trust (listen attentively, speak candidly, treat others respectfully), Dive Deep (operate at all levels, no task beneath you). Prepare 2-3 specific stories per LP. Generic 'I led a project' answers fail; interviewers want concrete details about what YOU specifically did.

Amazon SDE-1 interview loop structure (2024-2025): online assessment first (1.5 hours, 2 coding problems + a work simulation), then a phone screen (1 coding + LPs), then the loop with 4-5 interviews. Of those loop rounds, typically 2 are coding (one medium, one medium-hard), 1 is system design (lighter than senior-level), 1 is pure behavioral, and 1 is the Bar Raiser — a senior Amazonian from a different team whose job is to ensure the candidate raises the bar. The Bar Raiser has veto power over the entire loop. Coding questions tend to be LeetCode medium with a 'real-world' framing: 'we have a log file with these entries, find...' rather than abstract array problems.

Common Amazon coding patterns: graph traversal problems (BFS/DFS on grids, finding connected components), interval/scheduling problems, sliding window for log analysis, top-K problems with heaps, string manipulation with hash maps, simulation problems where you implement business logic step by step. They tend to avoid pure dynamic programming and very hard tree problems. The bar isn't whether you find the optimal solution — it's whether you communicate well, handle edge cases, and write production-quality code. Test your code with the examples. Use meaningful variable names. Handle empty inputs and null values.

Amazon behavioral question examples mapped to LPs: 'Tell me about a time you took on a significant risk' (Bias for Action, Ownership). 'Describe a time you had to make a decision with incomplete data' (Are Right A Lot, Bias for Action). 'Tell me about a time you went above and beyond for a customer' (Customer Obsession). 'Describe a project where you had to push back on a stakeholder' (Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit, Earn Trust). 'Tell me about your biggest failure' (Learn and Be Curious, Earn Trust). For each story, use STAR but front-load the Action — Amazon interviewers will interrupt and ask 'but what did YOU do specifically' if you say 'we'. Quantify outcomes when possible ('reduced latency by 40%', 'saved $200k/year').
